featuredImageAlt: The AFL Premier League standings on a phone, with rank, team, all-play record, and prize columns all fitting the screen — no sideways scrolling

changes.summary: The roster loading screen learned to breathe. Its rotating one-liners were flipping every 2.2 seconds — just enough time to read half a quip before the next one shoved it offstage. Each line now hangs around for a full five seconds, and the loader itself waits a beat longer before appearing so quick page loads skip the theatrics entirely.

The 'How It Works' tab clinging to the side of the Rosters page has been retired. It spent the spring walking newcomers through contract declarations, but with declaration season behind us it was just photobombing your roster — so the guided demo has left the building. The contract tools themselves haven't gone anywhere.

Dark mode stopped hiding half the league's rookies. The little circular player headshots wear their NFL team's colors behind them — great in theory, except a third of the league (Titans, Steelers, Bears, Seahawks, Texans...) wears colors that are basically midnight, so a guy like Cam Ward in a navy jersey on a navy chip on a dark page was just a rumor of a face. The chips now reach for each team's lighter brand color when the main one is too dark — Titans light blue, Steelers gold, Bears orange — and every chip puts a soft spotlight right where the player's head sits, brightest behind the face and deepening toward the rim. Every team's backdrop is held to a minimum brightness, enforced by a build-time guard so no future team-color tweak can sink a headshot back into the void. Light mode untouched; it was never the problem.

Schefter's daily Cut Watch dispatches were going straight to the website and skipping the group chat entirely — he'd type up 'Midwestside Connection faces the guillotine, 6 over the limit' for the site feed and then just... not hit send on GroupMe. Turns out the article pipeline only pings the chat for story types that define a promo, and Cut Watch never got one. Fixed: whenever at least one team is over the 22-man limit, the chat now gets the headline stat, how many players the league still needs to shed, the countdown to the August cutdown deadline, and a link to the full chopping block. On days when everyone's compliant, he keeps it to himself — no buzz for a non-story. And while we were in there, his editor dialed the column back from daily to a proper Sunday feature: one Cut Watch every Sunday morning of the cut window instead of thirty-three straight days of guillotine coverage. He also got a new source inside the league office: Schefter now knows which over-limit owners have already filed their auto-cut plans — those teams get a respectful nod for having their affairs in order, while the heat goes straight to anyone over the limit with nothing on file. He knows THAT you've made your picks, never WHICH picks — your cut list stays sealed. His chopping-block picks got smarter too: instead of just pointing at the cheapest contracts, likely cuts now come from a combined value ranking that blends redraft and dynasty ADP by contract length — a 1-year rental is judged purely on this season's draft boards, a 5-year keeper purely on dynasty boards. (Fixing that also uncovered that the 'dynasty ADP' feed had been a carbon copy of redraft data all along — now it actually pulls dynasty leagues.)

The contract-year chips on the Rosters page — the little blue '4' with its ticking '27d' deadline badge — were still dressed for daylight in dark mode, glowing like sticky notes on a midnight desk. They now wear proper dark-mode colors: translucent fills, readable text, and a deadline badge that shifts from amber to orange to red as the clock runs down without searing your retinas on the way.

The side menu's league-switch chevron now actually switches leagues. On theleague.us and afl-fantasy.com it used to strip the other league's address right out of its own link — so 'Switch to AFL' just reloaded the page you were already on. It now carries you straight to the same page on the other league's site, and the chevron shows for everyone instead of hiding unless you were logged in as a dual-league owner.

The 'whisper back' links in Schefter's GroupMe posts work now. Every clean link on the league domains — afl-fantasy.com/schefter/tip, theleague.us/rosters, all of them — was quietly being stamped page-not-found behind the scenes; most pages shrugged it off and displayed anyway, but the tip page's sign-in handoff couldn't, so tapping a tip link from GroupMe dead-ended on a 404. The routing now reports what actually happened, the sign-in bounce goes through, and the link even remembers which team's desk you were headed to after you log in. Bonus: a bare /schefter now drops you at the Schefter Report feed instead of a dead end.

Dark mode got a full sweep of the closet. The Admin Hub bug from earlier this week — styling against color tokens that don't actually exist, so everything silently rendered its light-mode fallback — turned out to have cousins in some forty files: white draft-queue rows on a dark draft room, lineup accordions and matchup pickers stuck with light text, homepage urgency chips glowing like flashlights, and a templates page whose headings had no color at all. Every one now runs through the real theme tokens, and a new build-time guard fails any future code that references a token that doesn't exist — so this whole class of bug is done recurring.

The Admin Hub finally respects dark mode — its cards were stuck glowing white on the dark background because they styled themselves against token names that don't exist — and the August Cutdown Report now has a card there, so the commissioner's audit trail is one click away instead of a memorized URL.

Claude Schefter no longer ghosts you after one bad draft. When his quality gate spiked a post for being too vague, the scanner still charged it against the day's posting budget — so one scrapped rumor at 7am could silence the rumor mill until midnight, tips waiting patiently the whole time. Budgets now only count posts that actually ship. And when the feed's been quiet for a week (or a subject hasn't come up all week), the bar for 'worth posting' drops — a scrappy 3/10 rumor beats radio silence.

The AFL draft order finally admits when it's done predicting. Once the NIT wrapped, the order was set in stone — but the homepage hero and the draft page kept calling it a 'predictor,' like a weatherman forecasting yesterday. Now the page flips to the official draft order (green badge and all) the moment the playoffs and NIT finish, the offseason hero says 'View Draft Order,' and the projected framing returns automatically when the new season kicks off.

The Draft Order page now knows what phase of the calendar it lives in. During the season it's a live prediction that moves with the standings; once the champion is crowned and the toilet bowl comp picks are settled, it wears an official badge instead of hedging; and now that the rookie draft is in the books, it says so plainly — final order as drafted, with the next prediction cycle firing up when the new season kicks off. The spring draft heroes got the same memo and link to 'View Draft Order' instead of a predictor that stopped predicting months ago.

Standings finally sit still on your phone — the Premier League tables (and every other standings view) used to shove the whole page sideways, so you had to swipe left and right just to read a record. Now the columns that matter — rank, team, and record — fit the screen, the rest tuck away until you've got the width for them, and the desktop layout is untouched.

AFL links that point back to MFL now actually point at the AFL. A pair of long-standing wiring mistakes had AFL pages borrowing TheLeague's MFL server for outbound links: the What's Next event buttons (live scoring, options pages) and the Playoffs page's live-scoring polls and league-options link were all built against TheLeague's www49 host — so an AFL owner clicking through could land on the wrong league entirely. Both now resolve the AFL's own MFL server from the league registry, every time.

Trade block tags on the AFL Rosters page no longer vanish after a reload — flagging a player now sticks visibly, and the tag only shows on the team that actually flagged him (not his conference twin).
